title: AI Fundamentals
description: Core AI, ML, and GenAI concepts every candidate should be able to explain clearly.

questions.title: Explain the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI.
questions.scenario: A product team at a fintech runs AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification. A recent review found that the team is choosing impressive models before proving that data quality and problem framing are sound; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI measured by precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided.
questions.expectedAnswer: the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification, the practical approach is the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from labeled tickets, transaction events, model scores, and human review outcomes, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated. the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI addresses that risk through the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from labeled tickets, transaction events, model scores, and human review outcomes. Implement the change with domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: Compare supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.

commonMistakes: Explaining the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided.
Ignoring the main risk: generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A product team at a fintech applied the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI in AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification. The baseline suffered from generic implementation that cannot be measured or operated, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI applied with clear problem framing, measurable validation, operational controls, and production feedback using domain datasets, baselines, monitoring, evaluation dashboards, and human review, then evaluated the change against precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: How would you explain model drift in a AI Fundamentals interview?
questions.scenario: A product team at a fintech runs AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification. A recent review found that the team is choosing impressive models before proving that data quality and problem framing are sound; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for model drift measured by precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided.
questions.expectedAnswer: model drift belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. In AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from labeled tickets, transaction events, model scores, and human review outcomes, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems. model drift addresses that risk through model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from labeled tickets, transaction events, model scores, and human review outcomes. Implement the change with OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in AI triage service for support tickets, fraud alerts, and document classification, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor precision/recall by class, review workload, and business loss avoided so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.
